summary.title: [WebGL Animation] Moving document instances retain a stale frustum bound
summary.one_line_description: The animated document stream can be culled against its first-frame positions after its instances have moved.

bug_1.file_locations: - /Users/sacino/fintrace/src/app/engine-network/Scene.tsx - function: `EvidenceScene`, document setup and `render`, Lines 305-312 and 722-747

issue_description.description: The scene changes every document instance matrix on every frame, but it neither disables frustum culling for the InstancedMesh nor refreshes its bounding sphere. Three.js computes an InstancedMesh bounding sphere when first needed and does not automatically update it after setMatrixAt calls. The renderer therefore continues testing the stream against its first-frame random distribution, even as documents travel from x = -13 to the gate and wrap to new positions. Depending on the initial random phases and later movement, the stale sphere can leave the camera frustum while current documents are visible, causing the entire incoming stream to disappear intermittently. Disable frustum culling for this continuously animated mesh, as the scene already does for dynamic thread buffers, or explicitly recompute its bounding sphere after all instance matrices are updated.
issue_description.expected_vs_actual: | Aspect | Expected | Actual | |--------|----------|--------| | **Framing** | Frustum checks follow the current document positions | Frustum checks use the first computed instance bound | | **Animation** | Visible documents remain rendered throughout each journey | The complete InstancedMesh can be culled while current instances are in view | | **Reliability** | Random starting phases do not affect stream visibility | Initial random placement determines the stale culling volume |

summary.title: [WebGL Lifecycle] Instanced document buffers are not released on unmount
summary.one_line_description: Scene cleanup omits InstancedMesh disposal, leaving its matrix and colour GPU attributes allocated.

issue_description.description: The cleanup disposes the document geometry, material and texture but never calls dispose on the InstancedMesh itself. Three.js allocates separate GPU attributes for documents.instanceMatrix and documents.instanceColor; these attributes are released by the InstancedMesh dispose event rather than by BufferGeometry.dispose. WebGLRenderer.dispose clears renderer state but does not dispatch that mesh event. Repeated route mounts can therefore retain two document buffers in abandoned contexts until browser garbage collection, violating the scene's exhaustive-disposal requirement and increasing GPU memory pressure. Call documents.dispose() during cleanup before disposing the renderer.
issue_description.code_example:
```typescript // File: /Users/sacino/fintrace/src/app/engine-network/Scene.tsx, Lines 989-1023
docGeometry.dispose() docMaterial.dispose() paperTexture.dispose()
renderer.dispose() mount.removeChild(renderer.domElement) ```
issue_description.expected_vs_actual: | Aspect | Expected | Actual | |--------|----------|--------| | **Cleanup** | Every scene-owned GPU buffer is explicitly released | Instanced matrix and colour buffers receive no dispose event | | **Navigation** | Repeated route mounts have stable GPU memory use | Each mount can leave document instance buffers pending context collection | | **Lifecycle Contract** | Unmount satisfies exhaustive Three.js disposal | Shared geometry is disposed, but the InstancedMesh-owned attributes are omitted |

summary.title: [Responsive fallback] Static gate jumps across the hero when WebGL appears
summary.one_line_description: The fixed, slice-cropped SVG gate sits far right on portrait viewports while the live compact Scene places the gate left of centre.

bug_3.file_locations: - /Users/sacino/fintrace/src/app/engine-network/Hero.tsx - component: `Hero`, Lines 30-50 - /Users/sacino/fintrace/src/app/engine-network/engine-network.css - selectors: `.eng-hero-fallback` and `.eng-hero-fallback svg`, Lines 352-364 - /Users/sacino/fintrace/src/app/engine-network/Scene.tsx - function: `EvidenceScene`, Lines 253-293

issue_description.description: The fallback always renders a 1440×900 SVG with its gate at x = 905 and uses preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMid slice", while the live Scene places compact-layout gates at world x = -0.7 and wide-layout gates with stageShiftFor(aspect). The fixed SVG is therefore cropped from both sides on portrait and tall viewports instead of adapting to the compact composition. At 390×900, the slice transform places the fallback gate centre at approximately x = 380 px (97% of the viewport), while the compact Scene projects its gate near x = 121 px (31%). At 900×1080, the fallback gate is approximately x = 672 px (75%) while the live gate is approximately x = 362 px (40%). The CSS fallback glow remains independently fixed at 63%, so it does not align with either compact position. Users see the gate jump hundreds of pixels during the 1.4-second cross-fade; when WebGL is unavailable, the nearly clipped fallback remains as the permanent hero. Align the fallback gate and glow with the same compact/wide positioning contract used by the Scene, using responsive fallback variants or equivalent aspect-aware SVG positioning.
issue_description.expected_vs_actual: | Aspect | Expected | Actual | |--------|----------|--------| | **Initial cross-fade** | Fallback and live gate occupy the same responsive region | Gate jumps by roughly 259 px at 390×900 and 310 px at 900×1080 | | **WebGL-off fallback** | Static hero preserves the compact left-of-centre composition | Gate is pushed to 97% at 390×900 and the constellation is heavily cropped | | **Glow alignment** | Fallback glow stays centred on the fallback gate | CSS glow stays at 63% while the cropped SVG gate and live Scene use different positions |
